title: I want to connect to a container from the Mac
body:
/kind feature

**Description**
I want to connect to a container from the Mac

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Start the server
```
podman run infinispan/server
```
2. Get the container IP 
```
podman inspect -f '{{range.NetworkSettings.Networks}}{{.IPAddress}}{{end}}' 78a25d147770
10.88.0.2
```
3. Try to access the container from the Mac
```
curl http://10.88.0.2:11222/console/welcome
Operation timed out
```
**Describe the results you received:**
`Operation timed out`

**Describe the results you expected:**
`<!doctype html><html lang=".....`

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```

```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md)**

Yes

comments.body: You cannot connect directly via IP. Since the VM and containers are started rootless by default there is no way to setup interfaces/routing rules for that. You have to use port forwarding `podman run -p ...`.

The interfaces and routing on Mac can be created by the user. Example:
```
sudo "create route"
podman machine start
sudo route -n add 10.88.0.0/16 10.88.0.1
```

I wonder if this is worth doing by default - seems relatively low-impact and easy to implement.

Can someone confirm if Docker Desktop does this?

I am using an outdated version of docker-machine with an outdated version of Virtual Machine. It was working before upgrading my MacBook to Montery

The steps are the following:
* Install: VirtualBox-6.1.22-144080-OSX.dmg
* Install vagrant and use the following image
```
Vagrant.configure("2") do |config|
  config.vm.box = "ubuntu/kinetic64"
  config.vm.network "private_network", ip: "192.168.56.6"
  config.vm.provision "shell", inline: <<-SHELL
    apt-get update
  SHELL
end
```
then:
```
vagrant up
eval $(docker-machine env default)
sudo route -n add 172.17.0.0/16 $(docker-machine ip default)
```

* Access the VM with `vagrant ssh` and install the latest docker version
* Change `/etc/sysctl.conf` adding `net.ipv4.ip_forward = 1`

Create the docker-machine
```
docker-machine create \
      --driver "generic" \
      --generic-ip-address 192.168.56.6 \
      --generic-ssh-user vagrant \
      --generic-ssh-key /path/to/private_key \
      --generic-ssh-port 22 \
      default
```

Reboot the VM.
from the terminal: `docker run -d httpd` and then `curl 172.17.0.2`

docker machine is deprecated please try docker desktop.

Anyway creating the interfaces on the host will do nothing, you need to configure the networking with qemu.

It is documented as a limitation

```
I cannot ping my containers
Docker Desktop for Mac can’t route traffic to containers.
```

https://docs.docker.com/desktop/mac/networking/
Hmm. Possible there's a reason they decided to stop. I'd still like to add this, but we should investigate further.

I have a lot of tests that rely on the assumption that I can ping the container.
If you would like to investigate and maybe create an experimental attribute for `podman`, I can run our test suite and provide feedback.

Thanks,
@diegolovison if docker has moved away from it, is it reasonable to keep this assumption in your test suite?

Hi @baude,
no, because we have a custom protocol that is called Hot Rod.
Hot Rod (client) needs to keep the mapping between the server ports.

This ( support Mac connect to the container ) feature can be experimental ( forever, not supported in production ) and be available only on rootfull ( if it is the case )

Also, I am a heavy user ( creating testing using containers ) and I believe both communities can benefit from this feature.

Just want to say I agree that this would be an awesome feature. It also would bring parity to builds on CI (linux) and our developer machines. We basically have the same issue with testing using containers.

Hi, you can have it working ( with docker, docker-machine and VirtualBox ) if you do the following

Create a `Vagrantfile`
```
Vagrant.configure("2") do |config|
  config.vm.box = "fedora/34-cloud-base"
  config.disksize.size = '100GB'
  config.vm.network "private_network", ip: "192.168.56.60"
  config.vm.network "forwarded_port", guest: 22, host: 2222, host_ip: "127.0.0.1", id: 'ssh'
  config.vm.synced_folder "/Users/dlovison/Documents/GitHub/", "/home/vagrant/github"
  config.vm.synced_folder "/Users/dlovison/Documents/vagrant_v2/volumes", "/home/vagrant/volumes"
  config.vm.synced_folder "/Users/dlovison/.m2", "/home/vagrant/.m2"
  config.vbguest.auto_update = false
  config.vm.provider :virtualbox do |vb|
    # vb.customize ["modifyvm", :id, "--cableconnected1", "on"]
    # vb.customize ["modifyvm", :id, "--natdnshostresolver1", "on"]
    vb.memory = 4048
    vb.cpus = 2
    vb.gui = false
  end
end
```
```
vagrant up
vagrant ssh
```
In the VM, change the following to and install docker using the docs https://docs.docker.com/engine/install/fedora/
```
net.ipv4.ip_forward=1
```
In the host machine, install
```
brew install docker-machine
```
Create a generic driver
```
docker-machine create \
      --driver "generic" \
      --generic-ip-address 192.168.56.60 \
      --generic-ssh-user vagrant \
      --generic-ssh-key /Users/dlovison/Documents/vagrant/fedora34/.vagrant/machines/default/virtualbox/private_key \
      --generic-ssh-port 22 \
      default
```
```
eval $(docker-machine env default)
sudo route -n add 172.17.0.0/16 192.168.56.60
docker run -d httpd
curl 172.17.0.2
```

You should repeat the following everytime you reboot your MacBook
```
eval $(docker-machine env default)
sudo route -n add 172.17.0.0/16 192.168.56.60
```

Following the example above you can access the machines by IP and Port in your MacBook.

Thanks
